nativeRange.habitat: The Red-Bar Anthias is typically found over open substrate near low reef structures and isolated coral heads, known as bommies, usually at depths of around 10 to 60 metres, though individuals have been recorded as deep as 91 metres. Juveniles may occur in shallower coastal reef environments. In the wild, this species tends to form small, loose aggregations along current-swept drop-offs and reef edges.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves, ledges, and overhangs

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton in the wild, including small crustaceans. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, cyclops, calanus, and over time may take high-quality flake or pellet foods. Small portions multiple times per day (3–4 feedings) are generally advised to support a high metabolism. A refugium cultivating live copepods and amphipods is commonly recommended as a supplemental food source.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish of similar size
avoidWith: other male Red-Bar Anthias (in smaller tanks)

summary: The Red-Bar Anthias is a peaceful, reef-safe planktivore from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its vivid pink-and-red coloration. It is generally recommended in a harem of one male with several females, and requires a well-established system with frequent daily feedings to thrive.
careNotes:
The Red-Bar Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant individual in a group will commonly transition to male if no male is present. Males display a light pink body with a distinctive vertical red bar across the mid-section; females tend to be more orange with a silvery belly and a red tail. Males are generally considered territorial toward other males of the same species and are not commonly housed together unless the tank is very large.

For a harem group, a tank of at least 500 L is widely considered the practical minimum, with 800 L or more preferred for a comfortable small group. A single specimen can be kept in a smaller system of around 265 L (70 gallons), though a group is generally preferred for the species' social well-being.

This species has a high metabolism and has been reported to decline quickly if underfed. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised, and a refugium seeding live copepods and amphipods is commonly recommended as a supplemental food source.

Anthias species have been documented jumping from open aquaria, so a tight-fitting lid or full cover is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered difficult and has not been routinely documented in home aquariums. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is absent. Broadcast spawning has been reported in large display systems, but raising the small pelagic larvae on a zooplankton diet is rarely successful outside dedicated breeder setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: tank size (70 gal single / 125 gal group), temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, peaceful temperament, reef-safe.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Family Anthiadidae, Order Perciformes), max adult size 14 cm TL, temperature range 22–27°C, depth 10–91 m (usually 10–60 m), Indo-Pacific distribution from East Africa to Samoa and the Line Islands, southern Japan to the Great Barrier Reef.

fish.breedingNotes: The Red-Bar Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is removed or absent. Captive spawning has been reported in large display systems, but larval rearing is rarely successful in home aquariums.
